questions.question: Describe two common challenges that arise with I/O operations in a parallel computing environment and briefly explain why they are problematic.
keyPoints: The 'I/O bottleneck' where the bandwidth of the storage system is insufficient to handle concurrent requests from many processors, leading to significant performance degradation.
Difficulty in maintaining data consistency when multiple processes are reading from and writing to the same file, which can lead to race conditions and corrupted data.

questions.question: Explain the concept of collective I/O. Describe how it improves performance and name one common API that implements this strategy.
keyPoints: Collective I/O is a strategy where I/O requests from multiple processes are combined and coordinated into larger, contiguous requests.
It improves performance by reducing the number of small, non-contiguous accesses, which are inefficient, and by leveraging the higher bandwidth of large, sequential data transfers.
The MPI-IO (Message Passing Interface - I/O) API is a common standard that implements collective I/O operations.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the strategies of data partitioning and two-phase I/O for managing parallel access to a single file. Discuss the advantages and potential drawbacks of each approach in a high-performance computing context.
keyPoints: Data partitioning involves dividing a file into distinct, non-overlapping regions assigned to different processes, minimizing contention but potentially leading to load imbalance if data access is not uniform.

Two-phase I/O (or collective I/O) involves a coordination phase where processes agree on access patterns, followed by an I/O phase where data is combined into large, efficient requests, improving bandwidth usage but adding coordination overhead.

An advantage of partitioning is its simplicity and low overhead for predictable access patterns; a drawback is its inflexibility for complex, non-uniform data access. An advantage of two-phase I/O is its high efficiency for contiguous access patterns; a drawback is the performance cost of the required coordination and communication between processes.
